data.prompt:
<execute-bead>
  <instructions>
You are executing one bead inside an isolated DDx execution worktree. The bead's <description> and <acceptance> are the completion contract — every AC must be provably satisfied by a specific code, test, or file you can point to after your commit.

## Step 0: size check

Too big if any holds:

- More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems.
- AC mixes design, implementation, integration tests, and docs as separate deliverables.
- Description names multiple feature-sized sub-pieces.
- More than ~500 lines across ~5+ files in unrelated packages.
- If the bead description exceeds 8000 bytes, treat Step 0 as a split-first pass.
- Auto-decomposition is capped at depth 2: root beads may split once, decomposed children once more; third-level splits must be rejected with an explanation.

If too big, decompose — do not attempt the work:

1. `ddx bead create` for each child slice (copy parent's labels and spec-id).
2. `ddx bead dep add <child-id> <parent-id>` to record edges.
3. `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.
4. Write `.ddx/executions/20260514T213246-62c83fdc/no_changes_rationale.txt` listing child IDs, then stop.

A clean decomposition is a success. Do not mix implementation with decomposition.

## How to work

- Read first. If the bead names files, specs, or prior beads, read them before editing — do not guess.
- Cross-reference each AC against concrete evidence (test name, file path, function) before committing. If you cannot point at it, it is not done.
- Run the project's test and lint commands before committing. **Do not commit red code** — fix failures first.
- Stage with `git add <specific-paths>`; never `git add -A` (the worktree may have unrelated WIP).
- Commit exactly once when green; conventional-commit subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. Stop after the commit.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's scope.
- If you cannot finish, write `.ddx/executions/20260514T213246-62c83fdc/no_changes_rationale.txt` (what is done, what blocks, what a follow-up needs) before exiting. No commit and no rationale ⇒ DDx records `no_evidence_produced`. A well-justified no_changes beats a bad commit.

## no_changes contract

`.ddx/executions/20260514T213246-62c83fdc/no_changes_rationale.txt` must contain one:

- `verification_command: <cmd>` — repo cwd; exit 0 closes, nonzero rejects.
- `status: open` + `reason: <retryable>` — open, smart retry.
- `status: proposed` + `reason: <operator needed>` — operator lane.
- `status: blocked` + `reason: <external blocker>` — blocked lane.

No pseudo-statuses. Bare rationales and `status: needs_investigation` reject.

To request orchestrator decomposition, add `orchestrator_action: decompose` alongside `status: open`.

## Reports

Freestanding artifacts (investigation reports, findings docs) go under `.ddx/executions/20260514T213246-62c83fdc/` (the per-attempt evidence directory under `.ddx/executions/`). **Never write reports to `/tmp` or any path outside the repository** — out-of-repo paths are invisible to the post-merge reviewer and cause BLOCK on missing evidence. If the bead names a specific in-repo path, use it; else default to `.ddx/executions/20260514T213246-62c83fdc/<short-name>.md`. Stage and commit the report alongside code.

## Mode and overrides

DDX_MODE=bead_execution: edit code/docs for bead AC. Only broad queue-steward default is overridden; tracker, merge-policy, verification, safety stay active. Bead description/AC override CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and project defaults.

## Review gate

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized; running it corrupts the bead queue.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other project-instructions files unless the bead asks.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and every AC is verified, stop. Return control to the orchestrator — do not keep exploring or testing.
  </instructions>
  <bead id="ddx-6ab52652">
    <title>update: refuse or warn before overwriting unstaged user changes to library/ files</title>
    <description>
PROBLEM
ddx update --force unconditionally overwrites files under library/ with their registry-version counterparts. Today this destroyed in-progress, uncommitted edits to library/skills/ddx/SKILL.md and library/skills/ddx/reference/work.md (a "Starting and monitoring workers in the background" section added earlier in the session that had not yet been committed). The user could not recover except via git stash (which itself had partial-apply issues). For an operator running ddx update --force as a remediation step (legacy-symlink fix), losing unrelated uncommitted authored work is silent and surprising.

ROOT CAUSE
cli/cmd/update.go's runUpdate path (around the file-copy stage) does not check whether target files have uncommitted modifications vs HEAD before overwriting. It treats library/ as fully owned by the registry. The --force flag bypasses any safety prompts that may have existed for non-force mode.

PROPOSED FIX
- Before overwriting any file under library/ (or any other update-target path), compute its git status. If the file is modified (in either the index or worktree relative to HEAD) and the new content differs from both HEAD and the current worktree, refuse the overwrite unless --force --discard-local is also passed.
- For --force without --discard-local: print a per-file diff summary and the explicit instruction "use --force --discard-local to overwrite anyway, or commit/stash first", exit non-zero.
- Document the new --discard-local flag in CHANGELOG and ddx update --help.
- Optionally: when overwriting under --force --discard-local, save the original to .ddx/update-backup/<timestamp>/<path> so the operator can recover.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not change update's git-merge behavior for the project's own files (not library/).
- Do not change the registry-fetch mechanism.

DEPS
None.
    </description>
    <acceptance>
1. TestUpdate_RefusesDirtyLibraryFile asserts ddx update --force exits non-zero when library/skills/ddx/SKILL.md is dirty vs HEAD (modified or staged) and content would differ post-overwrite. Output mentions --force --discard-local.
2. TestUpdate_AllowsCleanLibraryFile asserts ddx update --force overwrites a clean library/ file without prompting.
3. TestUpdate_DiscardLocalOverwritesDirty asserts ddx update --force --discard-local overwrites the dirty file and writes the original to .ddx/update-backup/<timestamp>/<path>.
4. TestUpdate_PartialDirtyBatchHaltsCleanly asserts that when multiple library/ files are slated for overwrite and even one is dirty without --discard-local, the entire update halts before any file is mutated (atomic refuse).
5. cd cli && go test ./cmd/... green.
6. lefthook run pre-commit passes.
    </acceptance>
    <labels>area:update, area:devx, kind:bug, phase:2</labels>
    <metadata base-rev="e7c5c8091bcc6243b29520ffe237fe1de3fd512a" bundle=".ddx/executions/20260514T213246-62c83fdc"/>
  </bead>
  <governing>
    <note>No governing references were pre-resolved. The bead description above is the primary contract. If it names files, specs, or prior beads, read those before editing. Ground decisions in what is already in the repository; do not guess.</note>
  </governing>
</execute-bead>

data.output:
All checks pass and the commit succeeded. 

The implementation adds:
- **`isUpdateTargetDirty`** — checks git status for a single file (ignores untracked `??` entries)
- **`enumerateSkillUpdateTargets`** — lists files `refreshShippedSkills` would write to
- **`collectDirtyUpdateTargets`** — collects dirty files from both skill targets and registry `entry.Files`
- **`backupUpdateFile`** — copies a file to `.ddx/update-backup/<timestamp>/<path>`
- **`--discard-local` flag** on `ddx update` — bypasses the guard (with backup)
- **Atomic refuse** in `performUpdate` — fails before ANY file is written if any target is dirty

Tests cover all four ACs: AC1 (refuse dirty), AC2 (allow clean), AC3 (discard-local with backup), AC4 (partial dirty halts entire update cleanly).
